slides.title: The Calculator App 🧮
slides.content:
Kotlin supports all the standard mathematical operators you learned in school:

- `+` Addition
- `-` Subtraction
- `*` Multiplication
- `/` Division
- `%` Modulus (Remainder)

slides.title: Basic Arithmetic ➕➖
slides.content: Using these operators is very straightforward. You can perform operations directly on numbers or on numeric variables.
slides.codeSnippet:
val score1 = 50
val score2 = 25

val total = score1 + score2
println(total) // Output: 75

println(10 * 3) // Output: 30

quizData.question: What is the output of `val result = 20 / 4; println(result)`?

slides.title: The Modulus Operator %
slides.content:
## Finding the Remainder

The `%` operator is called Modulus. It performs division, but instead of returning the result, it returns the **remainder**.

Example: `10 % 3`.
3 goes into 10 three times (9). The remainder is **1**.

quizData.question: What is the result of `14 % 5`?
quizData.explanation: 5 goes into 14 twice (10), leaving a remainder of 4.

slides.title: Augmented Assign 📈
slides.content:
Often you'll want to take a variable, add something to it, and save it back into the same variable.

Instead of writing `score = score + 5`, you can use the shorter **augmented assignment** operator `+=`.
slides.codeSnippet:
var score = 10 
score += 5   // Same as score = score + 5
score -= 2   // Same as score = score - 2

println(score) // Output will be 13
